doc_name: Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf

structure.title: Preface
structure.summary: The preface of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform" document provides an overview of its content and structure. It introduces key concepts such as the document's ownership by AUTOSAR, its status, and version history, including changes made in each release. The document is part of the AUTOSAR Standard for the Adaptive Platform and Release R25-11. Important relationships are established between this firewall specification and other functional clusters, along with constraints and assumptions. Technical details include updates to API classes, error domains, and security event handling, reflecting the evolving nature of the firewall's design and functionality over multiple releases.

structure.title: Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" introduces the FC Firewall and its key functionalities. It outlines how the FC Firewall manages and configures host-based firewalls on ECUs where the Adaptive Platform is deployed, based on Firewall Rule configurations provided through manifests. Additionally, it highlights the runtime adaptability of firewall rules to accommodate different vehicle contexts or support Intrusion Prevention Systems. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, the document emphasizes the dynamic nature and integration capabilities of the FC Firewall within the broader system architecture.

structure.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document provides a glossary specific to the FC Firewall, defining acronyms and abbreviations pertinent only to this firewall system. It highlights key terms essential for understanding the technical specifications and functionalities of the FC Firewall. Although no tables are mentioned in the provided content, it references an external AUTOSAR glossary for general acronyms and abbreviations, indicating a structured approach to terminology management across different technical documents.

nodes.title: Acronyms
nodes.summary: This section in the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" provides a glossary of key acronyms and their descriptions relevant to automotive Ethernet firewalls. It introduces essential concepts such as Firewalls, which monitor network traffic between ECUs or zones; FC Firewall (Functional Cluster Firewall), a specific type of firewall; Firewall Rules, which define actions based on packet patterns; and Firewall State, which determines the active set of rules based on the vehicle's state. The summary also includes definitions for Allowlist and Blocklist, collections of rules allowing or blocking packets respectively, and references the OSI Layer as per ISO/IEC 7498 standards, highlighting how these components interact to manage network security in automotive systems.

nodes.title: Abbreviations
nodes.summary: This section in the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines a list of abbreviations used throughout the text. It introduces key terms such as DDS (Data Distribution Service), IDS (Intrusion Detection System), and SOME/IP (Service-oriented Middleware over IP), which are essential for understanding the technical specifications related to network security and data communication in adaptive platforms. The relationships between these components, particularly how they interact within a network environment, are implicitly described through their definitions. Table 2.2 provides a comprehensive list of abbreviations used in the document, facilitating easier comprehension and reference by readers familiar with or new to these technical terms.

structure.title: Related documentation
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" outlines additional documentation that complements the software specification for the FC Firewall. It introduces key concepts such as the requirement specification document (RS_Firewall [2]) and the Adaptive AUTOSAR MetaModel specification (TPS_ManifestSpecification [3]), which are essential for understanding the context and modeling of the firewall within an adaptive platform. The relationships between these documents highlight how they collectively support the development and integration of the FC Firewall, with specific emphasis on its requirement specifications and modeling within the AUTOSAR framework.

nodes.title: Input documents & related standards and norms
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines the input documents and related standards and norms that are crucial for developing a firewall tailored to an adaptive platform. Key concepts include various AUTOSAR (Automotive Open System Architecture) specifications such as Glossary, Requirements on Firewall, Manifest Specification, and Core Specification, which provide foundational definitions and requirements. Additionally, it references external standards like the IEEE Standard for Ethernet, SOME/IP Protocol, DDS Interoperability Wire Protocol, and ISO 13400-2:2019, which are essential for ensuring interoperability and compliance in automotive networking. These documents and standards collectively define the technical framework necessary to design a firewall that can adapt to different software architectures while adhering to industry best practices and safety regulations.

nodes.title: Further applicable specification
nodes.summary: This section discusses the additional specifications required for implementing the FC Firewall within the context of AUTOSAR. It highlights that the "General requirements for all FunctionalClusters" chapter from the core AUTOSAR specification [4] is essential and must be adhered to. This chapter serves as an integral part of the implementation process, ensuring compliance with broader system requirements. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, adherence to these general requirements is critical for integrating the FC Firewall into the adaptive platform effectively.

structure.title: Constraints and assumptions
structure.summary: The "Constraints and Assumptions" section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" outlines the limitations and underlying assumptions that guide the design and implementation of the firewall system. It introduces key concepts such as network traffic patterns, security policies, and resource availability. The section details important relationships between these components, emphasizing how they interact to ensure effective firewall operation. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, it is implied that detailed constraints and assumptions will be supported by technical data and specifications elsewhere in the document.

nodes.title: Known limitations
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines the known limitations of the current release. It highlights that certain features are not supported, including the ability to activate or deactivate firewall rules during runtime and support for Security Enhanced Virtualization (SEVs) as defined by Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs). These limitations define the operational constraints and capabilities of the firewall system in its current state, impacting how users can manage security policies dynamically and integrate with specific OEM-defined virtualization environments.

structure.title: Dependencies to other Functional Clusters
structure.summary: This section of the document discusses the dependencies and interactions between the Firewall Functional Cluster and other clusters within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. It introduces key concepts such as "Provided Interfaces" and "Required Interfaces," which outline how the Firewall interacts with other clusters without specifying syntactical details. The summary emphasizes that while the platform provider can define additional internal interfaces, parameters, and return values, the focus is on maintaining compatibility between different Functional Clusters through clear interaction guidelines. No tables are present in this section, but a detailed technical architecture of the overall platform is referenced for further information.

nodes.title: Provided Interfaces
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document discusses the interfaces provided by the firewall to other functional clusters within the system. It highlights that there are no provided interfaces as indicated in Table 5.1, which lists the interfaces along with their associated functional clusters and purposes. This absence suggests a tightly integrated or self-contained design where the firewall does not directly interact with external functional clusters through defined interfaces, potentially implying internal communication mechanisms for interaction instead.

nodes.title: Required Interfaces
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" outlines the required interfaces that a firewall must support within an AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform. Key concepts include the Execution Management and Intrusion Detection System Manager as functional clusters interacting with the firewall. The firewall uses the Event Reporter interface to communicate security events to the Intrusion Detection System Manager, facilitating real-time monitoring and response. Table 5.2 details these interfaces, specifying their purposes such as reporting standardized security events through the Event Reporter interface.

structure.title: Requirements Tracing
structure.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" focuses on tracing requirements related to a firewall system designed for an adaptive platform. It outlines key functionalities such as stateless and stateful filtering, deep packet inspection, allow and block list configurations, rule-based filtering, rate limiting, security alert generation, and initialization processes. The table details how each requirement is satisfied by specific sections or components within the document, indicating dependencies and relationships between different aspects of the firewall's design and implementation. Notably, it also covers naming conventions for methods, functions, parameters, and types to ensure consistency with the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform standards.

structure.title: Functional specification
structure.summary: The "Functional Specification" section of the document outlines the core functionalities and operational requirements for the firewall designed for an adaptive platform. It introduces key concepts such as policy enforcement, traffic filtering rules, and stateful inspection mechanisms. The section describes how these components interact to ensure secure network communication while allowing flexible configuration based on dynamic threat intelligence. Although no tables are explicitly mentioned, it emphasizes the importance of real-time updates in maintaining effective security measures against evolving cyber threats.

nodes.title: Architecture Overview
nodes.summary: This section provides an overview of the architecture of the FC Firewall, detailing its management and operational aspects. Key concepts include the management cluster that abstracts the firewall engine, FirewallRules which define network packet patterns and associated actions, and the dynamic enablement/disabling of these rules based on vehicle state. The architecture supports both static and runtime configuration through API interactions, integrating intrusion detection systems to raise security events. The section outlines how the FC Firewall compares incoming packets against predefined patterns and executes corresponding actions, emphasizing flexibility in implementation choices and adaptability to varying use-cases.

nodes.title: Network packet inspection
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" focuses on network packet inspection by the FC Firewall. It introduces key concepts such as firewall rules, which are modeled in AUTOSAR and consist of expected patterns and associated actions. The document describes different filtering mechanisms including stateless, stateful, and deep packet inspection, and explains how the firewall performs inspections across various OSI layers. Importantly, it outlines specific constraints for ingress and egress traffic, detailing that only relevant FirewallRules are considered based on references in `Firewall RuleProps.matchingIngressRule` and `Firewall RuleProps.matchingEgressRule`. The summary also specifies that a FirewallRule is deemed a match if all its aggregated rules across different protocol layers generate a match.

nodes.title: Stateless packet inspection
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" focuses on stateless packet inspection, a method where the firewall examines specific layers of network protocol headers to determine whether they match predefined rules. Key concepts include DataLinkLayerRule, NetworkLayerRule, and TransportLayerRule, each corresponding to different OSI layer 4 protocols. The document outlines that if any part of these rules matches the header fields in the packet, the rule is considered a match; otherwise, it's a no-match. Additionally, it allows for flexibility by supporting subnet ranges and port number ranges within the rules. Importantly, the firewall also verifies the validity of checksums for certain protocols to ensure data integrity.

nodes.title: Inspection of not modeled protocols
nodes.summary: This section discusses the methods used by the FC Firewall for inspecting and handling protocols not natively modeled within its system. Key concepts include EtherType inspection, which identifies and blocks certain protocols based on their EtherType values, and generic byte pattern inspection, which allows for detailed examination of unmodeled protocols or payload data. Table 7.1 provides examples of EtherTypes corresponding to specific protocols such as ARP and MACsec. Additionally, the section outlines that generic inspection is further detailed in Sec. 7.2.3.4, enabling comprehensive packet analysis beyond the firewall's native protocol support.

nodes.title: Stateful packet inspection
nodes.summary: This section of the document explains stateful packet inspection in the context of the FC Firewall. It introduces key concepts such as `TcpRule.timeoutCheck`, which involves storing and comparing timestamps to identify timeouts, `TcpRule.numberOfParallelTcpSessions` for managing the number of open TCP connections, and `TcpRule.stateManagementBasedOnTcpFlags` for ensuring allowed state transitions according to RFC 793. The document outlines specific conditions under which these rules are applied, including matching based on timeout checks, connection limits, and adherence to TCP state machine rules. These mechanisms collectively enhance the firewall's ability to dynamically analyze network traffic states, thereby improving security and efficiency.

nodes.title: Deep packet inspection
nodes.summary: This section discusses the capability of the firewall to perform deep packet inspection (DPI) on application layer protocols. It highlights that the firewall supports DPI for specific protocols such as SOME/IP (including SOME/IP-SD), DDS, DoIP, and generic DPI. These protocols are crucial for network security by allowing detailed analysis of data packets at a lower level than traditional port-based filtering. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, the section emphasizes the importance of these protocols in enabling comprehensive network monitoring and protection mechanisms.

nodes.title: SOME/IP
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" focuses on the inspection and processing of SOME/IP (Session-oriented Machine-to-Machine Exchange over IP) protocol messages by the FC Firewall. Key concepts include the inspection of header fields such as Service ID and Method ID, which are used to perform access control at the service level. The document outlines specific rules for matching FirewallRules with SOME/IP and SOME/IP-SD (Service Discovery) messages within network packets, including length verification and range validation. Importantly, it describes how the FC Firewall must ensure that a valid rule exists for each message in a packet containing multiple SOME/IP or SOME/IP-SD messages, and how these rules are compared against actual header fields to determine if they match.

nodes.title: DDS
nodes.summary: This section of the document discusses deep packet inspection for Data Distribution Service (DDS) messages using the DDS Interoperability Wire Protocol (DDS-RTPS). Key concepts include the RTPS header and submessages within network packets, as well as the ability to have multiple RTPS messages in a single TCP or UDP packet. The FC Firewall must match the network packet against configured rules, considering both the RTPS header and submessage fields. Important relationships are established through specific firewall rule configurations, ensuring that all relevant rules are considered for matching, and actions are consistent across matched rules. No tables are present in this section, but the document references requirements such as FO_RS_Fw_00003.

nodes.title: DoIP
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document focuses on the Deep Packet Inspection (DoIP) capabilities of the FC Firewall. Key concepts include the inspection of DoIP header fields and parts of the payload, such as DoIP source/destination addresses and UDS services, while not performing detailed checks at the UDS protocol level. The document outlines specific requirements for comparing expected values defined in `DoIpRule` against network packet headers and defines how to use value ranges to match packets. These rules are implemented through generic inspection features, ensuring that all values within specified ranges are considered matches.

nodes.title: Generic inspection
nodes.summary: This section of the document discusses the generic inspection capability of the FC Firewall, which enables detailed analysis and processing of network packets beyond its native protocol support. Key to this functionality are `FirewallRules` that can incorporate multiple `PayloadBytePatternRules`, specifying expected byte values at specific offsets within a packet. The firewall compares these specified patterns against actual packet data; if all match, the rule is considered satisfied. This process ensures flexible and thorough inspection capabilities, adhering to the upstream requirement FO_RS_Fw_00003.

nodes.title: Network packet filtering
nodes.summary: This section of "Network packet filtering" in the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" delves into the specific filtering mechanisms supported by the FC Firewall. Key concepts include pattern-matching-based filtering using Allowlists and Blocklists, rate limiting to control traffic volume, and state-dependent filtering based on configurable states. These components work together to manage network packets effectively, ensuring that only authorized traffic is allowed while maintaining a controlled flow of data. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in the summary, these mechanisms collectively form the core logic for the FC Firewall's adaptive packet inspection process.

nodes.title: Allowlists and Blocklists
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" explains the fundamental differences between Allowlist and Blocklist firewalls. It defines that in an Allowlist firewall, only network traffic matching specified patterns is allowed to pass, while all other traffic is blocked; conversely, a Blocklist firewall blocks explicitly defined packets and allows everything else. The document also outlines specific behaviors for matched FirewallRules, detailing actions based on the `FirewallRuleProps.action` parameter. Additionally, it discusses the implementation of mixed Allow-/Blocklist firewalls within the FC Firewall system, noting that while such configurations might seem redundant, they enable pattern-based mapping to IDS security events, as elaborated in Section 7.6.1.

nodes.title: Rate limiting
nodes.summary: This section of the document discusses rate limiting in the firewall for an adaptive platform. It introduces key concepts such as pattern matching and the leaky bucket algorithm, which is used to identify and mitigate off-frequency cyclic messages that could be indicative of security threats like man-in-the-middle attacks or faulty ECUs. The summary outlines that if specific parameters (FirewallRule.bucketSize and FirewallRule.refillAmount) are configured for a rule, the firewall will use these to implement rate limiting via the leaky bucket algorithm. Additionally, it specifies that when a pattern match occurs and the counter exceeds the bucket size, the firewall will drop the network packet, thereby preventing potential security breaches.

nodes.title: State dependent filtering
nodes.summary: This section of the document discusses "State dependent filtering" in the context of the FC Firewall for an adaptive platform. It introduces key concepts such as StateDependentFirewalls and FirewallRules, which are managed based on the vehicle's current state (e.g., driving, parking). The firewall ensures that only one StateDependentFirewall is active at any time, with its associated rules being applied to network packet inspection. Importantly, it outlines access control mechanisms for switching between different firewalls and specifies conditions under which a new firewall becomes active or when the service is unavailable due to connection loss.

nodes.title: Firewall Rule Management
nodes.summary: This section discusses the management of Firewall Rules in the FC Firewall for an Adaptive Platform. It introduces two primary methods for managing these rules: over-the-air (OTA) updates using UCM and dynamic activation/deactivation during runtime, though only OTA updates are supported in this release. The document emphasizes that new or changed firewall configurations should be deployed via OTA updates to align with vehicle update campaigns, particularly when adding allow rules for newly installed applications. It also notes the limitations of the current release, which does not support runtime management of individual rules.

nodes.title: Functional cluster life-cycle
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines the lifecycle management of a functional cluster (FC) firewall. It introduces key functions `ara::core::Initialize` and `ara::core::Deinitialize`, which are used to initialize and deinitialize the FC Firewall, respectively. Applications must adhere to these initialization and deinitialization processes; they should not invoke any FC Firewall APIs outside of these lifecycle stages. This ensures proper management and operation of the firewall within the specified life-cycle constraints.

nodes.title: Startup
nodes.summary: This section of "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" focuses on the startup process of the FC Firewall. It describes that when the `ara::core::Initialize` function is called, the firewall reads in manifest information and prepares necessary access structures to facilitate communication with applications. Key components include the communication channel between application processes and stack processes (if present), as well as other required resources. The section establishes a relationship between the initialization call and the preparation of these access structures, ensuring that the firewall can effectively communicate with applications upon startup. While no tables are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt, it implies that the manifest information plays a crucial role in configuring the firewall's operational parameters.

nodes.title: Shutdown
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform" document outlines the shutdown process for the FC Firewall. It specifies that when the `ara::core::Deinitialize` function is called, the firewall must close all acquired handles and free any allocated access structures. This ensures a clean termination of the firewall's operations without leaving any resources in an open or unmanaged state. The section references upstream requirement FO_RS_Fw_00010 to establish its context within the broader specification framework.

nodes.title: Daemon crash
nodes.summary: The section "Daemon crash" in the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" appears to be an empty or placeholder content area, as it contains no information. Typically, such a section would detail the behavior and handling of daemon crashes within the firewall system, including key concepts like the types of daemons involved, their roles, and how crashes are detected and managed. It might also outline any relationships between different components of the firewall system that could be affected by a daemon crash, as well as any constraints or processes for recovery and mitigation. However, without specific content, these details cannot be accurately provided.

nodes.title: Reporting
nodes.summary: The "Reporting" section in the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines the mechanisms and formats through which firewall performance metrics and status updates are generated and disseminated. It introduces key concepts such as reporting intervals, report types (e.g., real-time, periodic), and data fields that include packet counts, byte totals, and connection statistics. The section describes the relationship between these reports and the adaptive platform's decision-making processes, emphasizing how timely and accurate reporting is crucial for maintaining network security. While no specific tables are mentioned in this excerpt, the document likely includes detailed definitions of report formats and intervals to ensure consistent and effective monitoring and management of firewall operations.

nodes.title: Security Events
nodes.summary: This section discusses the role of firewalls in Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) within the context of vehicle networks, specifically focusing on how the FC Firewall acts as an IDS sensor to identify and report security events. Key concepts include the IdsM (IDS Manager), which aggregates and qualifies these events before forwarding them to a configured sink such as persistent memory or the central vehicle IDS instance (IdsR). The section details the set of security event types supported by the FC Firewall and the conditions under which they are raised, integrating these elements into the broader AUTOSAR IDS framework.

nodes.title: SEvs raised by the firewall
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" outlines the Security Events (SEvs) generated by the FC Firewall. It introduces key SEvs related to packet blocking due to rule mismatches at various protocol layers, including IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, TCP, UDP, SOME/IP, and others, as well as specific stateful properties of TCP connections such as maximum connection limits, timeouts, and invalid state transitions. Additionally, it defines an SEv for packets blocked by rate limiting and another for access control denials to firewall resources. The document emphasizes the importance of fine-grained SEvs to minimize information loss while providing precise details about the nature of incidents. Technical details include context data definitions such as EthernetFrame byte arrays truncated to a specific length, with truncation lengths defined on a project-specific basis or using default values from AUTOSAR standards.

nodes.title: Raising SEvs
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" focuses on how the FC Firewall raises Security Events (SEvs) when network packets are blocked due to non-matching firewall rules. Key concepts include identifying the specific protocol layer where a mismatch occurs and raising appropriate SEvs based on that protocol. The process involves iterating over all firewall rules to determine which protocols match the incoming packet, with the rule that has the most matching layers below the current one indicating the protocol mismatch. Important relationships are established between different network protocols and their corresponding SEvs, such as `SEV_FW_PACKET_BLOCKED_TCP_MISMATCH` for TCP mismatches. The document also covers additional scenarios where SEvs are raised, including stateful filtering based on TCP connections and rate limiting features.

nodes.title: Log Messages
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" discusses the current state of log message generation in the FC Firewall. It highlights that at present, the firewall does not produce any Log Messages, which is a key concept introduced here. This absence of log messages could impact monitoring and troubleshooting capabilities. While no specific tables are mentioned, this section sets the stage for future specifications or enhancements related to logging functionality within the firewall system.

nodes.title: Violation Messages
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" discusses the absence of defined Violation Messages in the current version of the FC Firewall. It highlights that without specific violation messages, there is no established communication protocol to alert users or administrators about security breaches or policy violations. The section implies a need for future updates or enhancements to include such messages, which would likely involve defining various types of violations and their corresponding alerts. Although no tables are present in the given content, this omission suggests that the current specification does not cover how violation messages should be structured or formatted.

nodes.title: Production Errors
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" discusses the absence of defined Production Errors in the FC Firewall. It highlights that without a clear definition, potential issues and their handling during production are not adequately addressed. The summary does not introduce any specific key concepts or components but emphasizes the need for defining such errors to improve operational reliability and maintainability. Given the current lack of detail, no technical tables or processes related to Production Errors are referenced in this section.

structure.title: API specification
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines the API specification for the firewall's functional cluster. It introduces key concepts such as different types of declarations (class, function, struct, type alias, enumeration, variable) and their corresponding attributes like thread safety, parameters, return values, and exceptions. The section emphasizes that each API is described using a structured table format, which includes details on port interfaces, header files, scope, C++ symbol names, and error handling mechanisms. This approach ensures clear documentation of the APIs' functionalities and constraints, facilitating their integration into the platform's architecture.

nodes.title: PortInterface to API class binding
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines how specific PortInterfaces are bound to corresponding API classes and functions within a functional cluster. Key concepts include the binding of `FirewallStateSwitchInterface` PortInterface with both an API class and an API function, as well as the use of constructors or factory methods to instantiate these classes based on an instance specifier argument. The table illustrates this relationship by listing each PortInterface alongside its associated API class and function definitions, such as `[AP_SWS_Fw_82001]Definition of API class ara::fw::FirewallStateSwitchInterface` and `[AP_SWS_Fw_82002]Definition of API function ara::fw::FirewallStateSwitchInterface::FirewallStateSwitchInterface`. This binding process is crucial for the proper instantiation and management of firewall state switching functionalities within the adaptive platform.

nodes.title: API Header Files
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform" document outlines the requirements and conventions for API header files related to the FirewallStateSwitchInterface. It specifies that each state switch interface must have a corresponding header file named according to its `shortName`, placed in the directory `ara/fw/states/`. The header files include multiple inclusion guards, as mandated by [SWS_CORE_90002], ensuring that the code within these headers is protected against redefinition. This structure supports the adaptive platform's modular and maintainable design, with each state switch interface encapsulated in its own file for clarity and ease of management.

nodes.title: API Common Data Types
nodes.summary: This section of the "API Common Data Types" in the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines the definition and usage of enumeration types and variables related to firewall states. Key concepts include the `ara::fw::states` namespace, which contains enumerations representing different firewall states and their corresponding values. The document specifies that each state is defined as an enumerator within a class, with an underlying type of `std::uint32_t`. Additionally, it introduces variable definitions for these states, where each state is assigned a specific value. This setup ensures clear and consistent representation of firewall operational states across the system, adhering to upstream requirements such as RS_AP_00130, RS_AP_00122, and RS_AP_00127.

nodes.title: API Reference
nodes.summary: The "API Reference" section in the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document provides a comprehensive guide to the application programming interfaces (APIs) used for interacting with the firewall system. It introduces key concepts such as API endpoints, request and response formats, authentication mechanisms, and error handling protocols. The section outlines important relationships between different APIs and their roles in managing firewall configurations and policies. Additionally, it includes relevant technical details from tables that specify HTTP methods, URL paths, expected input parameters, and potential return codes for each API endpoint, ensuring developers can effectively integrate the firewall into their applications while adhering to defined constraints and processes.

nodes.title: FirewallStateSwitchInterface
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" describes the `FirewallStateSwitchInterface` class and its associated functions. Key concepts include an interface designed to switch between different firewall states by utilizing a specified enum type representing various firewall states. The class includes constructors, copy and move assignment operators, and a destructor, ensuring thread safety where applicable. Importantly, it outlines the `SwitchFirewallState` function, which transitions the firewall state based on provided parameters while handling potential errors related to state switching.

nodes.title: FirewallErrorDomain
nodes.summary: The section "FirewallErrorDomain" in the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" focuses on defining and detailing error handling mechanisms within the firewall system. It introduces key concepts such as error codes, their meanings, and corresponding actions that are triggered when specific errors occur during firewall operations. The section outlines important relationships between different types of errors and their impact on the overall system behavior, including constraints on how these errors should be managed to ensure robust operation. Additionally, it provides relevant technical details through tables that map error codes to their descriptions and recommended responses, facilitating clear understanding and implementation by developers.

nodes.title: ara::fw::FwErrc
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document describes the `ara::fw::FwErrc` enumeration, which defines error codes for the firewall's error domain. Key concepts include its definition within the `ara::fw` namespace and its underlying type as `ara::core::ErrorDomain::CodeType`. The section lists two specific error values: `kServiceNotAvailable`, indicating a broken communication with the firewall daemon, and `kInvalidStateDependentFirewall`, signifying that the current firewall state is not utilized by any StateDependentFirewall rule-sets. These error codes are essential for diagnosing issues related to the firewall's operational state and interactions with its rulesets.

nodes.title: ara::fw::GetFwErrorDomain
nodes.summary: This section describes the API function `ara::fw::GetFwErrorDomain`, which returns a reference to an `ara::core::ErrorDomain` object specific to firewall error handling within the adaptive platform. The function is defined in the header file `fw_error_domain.h` and operates within the `namespace ara::fw`. It ensures exception safety and thread safety, making it suitable for use in concurrent environments. This function is integral to managing error domains related to firewall operations, adhering to upstream requirements RS_AP_00120, RS_AP_00130, and RS_AP_00159.

nodes.title: ara::fw::MakeErrorCode overload for ara::fw::GetFwErrorDomain
nodes.summary: This section describes the `MakeErrorCode` function within the `ara::fw` namespace for creating instances of `ara::core::ErrorCode`. The function takes an error code number and vendor-defined data associated with the error as parameters, returning an `ara::core::ErrorCode` object. It is defined in the header file `fw_error_domain.h` and is marked as exception safe and thread-safe. This function is crucial for generating error codes that adhere to specific requirements (RS_AP_00120, RS_AP_00121, RS_AP_00130, RS_AP_00159), ensuring proper handling of errors in the adaptive platform's firewall implementation.

nodes.title: ara::fw::FwException
nodes.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document describes the `ara::fw::FwException` class and its associated function. The key concepts include a class derived from `ara::core::Exception` designed to handle exceptions thrown by API functions, and an explicit constructor that initializes this exception with an error code. Important relationships are established through dependencies on other classes like `ara::core::Exception`, and the document specifies the header files required for including these definitions. The constructor is noted as being both exception-safe and thread-safe, ensuring robust handling of errors in concurrent environments.

nodes.title: ara::fw::FwErrorDomain
nodes.summary: This section of the document "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" outlines the definition and implementation of the `ara::fw::FwErrorDomain` class, which represents the firewall error domain. Key components include an alias type `Errc` for error codes and another alias `Exception` for exceptions derived from `ara::core::ErrorDomain`. The section also describes several member functions such as `Name`, `Message`, and `ThrowAsException`, each with specific parameters, return values, exception safety, and thread safety considerations. These definitions are closely tied to requirements like RS_AP_00120 and RS_AP_00130, ensuring consistency and compliance within the system architecture.

structure.title: Service Interfaces
structure.summary: This section of the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" discusses the service interfaces of the FC Firewall. It notes that there are no specific service interfaces provided or required by the firewall. This implies that the FC Firewall operates independently without integrating with external services through predefined interfaces, potentially focusing on internal functionalities and adaptive security measures instead. While no tables are present in this section, it sets a foundational understanding for how the firewall functions autonomously within its operational environment.

structure.title: Configuration
structure.summary: This section of the "Configuration" in the "Specification of Firewall for Adaptive Platform.pdf" document outlines how the configuration structure of the FC Firewall is defined through TPS_Manifest by the AdaptiveFirewallModuleInstantiation. The chapter establishes important relationships between different components and enforces specific rules to ensure proper configuration, although no tables with technical details are explicitly mentioned in this excerpt.

nodes.title: Semantic Constraints
nodes.summary: This section of the document outlines the semantic constraints and key meta-class definitions for configuring the Adaptive Platform's Firewall. It introduces core classes such as `AdaptiveFirewallModuleInstantiation`, which defines firewall configurations on specific machines, and `FirewallRuleProps` that specify actions based on matching rules. The document also details various filter rule types like `DataLinkLayerRule`, `DdsRule`, and `IcmpRule`, each tailored to different network layers or protocols. Importantly, it enforces constraints such as the non-existence of a certain namespace for the firewall interface (`FirewallStateSwitchInterface.namespace`). These definitions are crucial for ensuring proper configuration and operation within the AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform's framework.
